title: The neuroscience of why eye contact feels threatening after abuse
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
Your eyes are not a weapon. But your brain doesn't know that yet. 

 I remember the first time someone looked at me with soft eyes after years of walking...
htmlContent:
Your eyes are not a weapon. But your brain doesn't know that yet.

I remember the first time someone looked at me with soft eyes after years of walking through hell. My throat closed. My chest went tight. My vision actually narrowed, like I was looking down a tunnel. And I thought: what the hell is wrong with me?

Here's the thing. Nothing was wrong with me. My nervous system was doing exactly what it had learned to do. Eye contact after abuse is not neutral. It's not just "looking at someone." For a brain that has been shaped by threat, eye contact registers as a predator's gaze. It triggers the same ancient circuits that fire when a deer spots a wolf.

This is not about being shy. It's not social anxiety. It's not a character flaw or a sign that you are broken. It's neuroscience. And once you understand how this works, you can stop blaming yourself for something your body learned in order to survive.

Let me walk you through what's actually happening in your brain and body when you try to hold someone's gaze and everything inside you screams look away.

The polyvagal theory explains why eye contact feels like danger

Stephen Porges is the researcher who developed polyvagal theory. His work changed how we understand the autonomic nervous system. And it changed how I understand my own inability to look people in the eye without feeling like I might shatter.

Here's the short version. Your nervous system has three main states. Ventral vagal is your social engagement state. This is where you feel safe, connected, and able to be present with another person. Your eyes can meet theirs. Your voice can find its natural pitch. Your face can relax into an expression that says I am here and I am okay.

Then there is sympathetic activation. Fight or flight. Your pupils dilate. Your heart rate climbs. Your field of vision narrows so you can focus on the threat. In this state, eye contact becomes a challenge. It's a stare-down. It's a contest. It's not connection. It's vigilance.

And then there is dorsal vagal shutdown. Freeze. Collapse. Dissociation. In this state, eye contact might feel impossible. You might look through someone rather than at them. Your eyes go flat. Your face goes still. You are not there anymore.

Let me tell you something that took me years to understand. The brain doesn't store trauma the way it stores your grocery list. It doesn't file it neatly in the hippocampus with a date stamp and a narrative arc. Trauma gets fragmented. Scattered across neural networks like shrapnel. A sound here. A body sensation there. A feeling with no story attached to it.

Bessel van der Kolk's research showed this clearly - traumatic memories live in the body, in the right hemisphere, in the amygdala. They bypass the language centers entirely. That's why you can't "think" your way out of a flashback. That's why someone can tell you "you're safe now" and your body doesn't believe a word of it.

Notice what's happening in your body right now. Is there tightness somewhere? A heaviness? Maybe a numbness that feels like nothing but is actually something. Your body has been holding this story for you. It's been doing that job faithfully, even when it cost you everything.

For someone who has survived abuse, the nervous system learns that other people are not safe. Especially people who look at you. Especially eyes that hold yours. Because in the context of abuse, eye contact often preceded pain. It was the moment before the hit, the demand, the violation. The brain encoded that sequence: eyes lock, danger follows.

So now, years later, when someone looks at you with warmth or curiosity or even love, your body doesn't know the difference. It sees a predator. It prepares for impact.

Wild, right? Your body is trying to protect you from a threat that no longer exists. But the wiring is still there.

Your brain's alarm system is stuck on high alert

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. He talks about the concept of incomplete survival responses. When you were abused, your body mobilized to fight or flee. But you couldn't. You had to stay still. You had to endure. So that energy never discharged. It got locked into your tissues, your muscles, your nervous system.

Eye contact becomes a trigger because it was part of the original threat. Your brain doesn't process time the way you do. It doesn't say "that was ten years ago, I am safe now." It says "that pattern is present, I must activate."

So when you meet someone's gaze, your amygdala fires. Your hypothalamus kicks in. Your adrenal glands dump cortisol and adrenaline into your bloodstream. Your heart rate spikes. Your breathing gets shallow. Your muscles tense. You might feel heat in your face or cold in your hands. You might want to look away, look down, look anywhere else.

This is not weakness. This is a biological response that kept you alive. Your body is not your enemy. It is a faithful soldier that learned a terrible lesson and refuses to forget it.

But here is where it gets tricky. The same people who tell you to "just make eye contact" or "look people in the eye to build trust" are giving you advice that can actually retraumatize you. They don't understand that for some of us, eye contact is not a social skill. It is a threat assessment.

I tried forcing myself for years. I would lock eyes with someone and count to five in my head while my stomach turned inside out. I thought if I just did it enough, I would get used to it. I would desensitize. I would become normal.

It didn't work. It made things worse. Because I was overriding my own nervous system's signal. I was telling my body "you are wrong, this is safe" when my body knew better. And my body responded by turning up the volume.

If you have tried to force yourself to make eye contact and felt worse afterward, you are not failing. You are listening to the wrong instruction manual.

How to work with your nervous system instead of against it

Recovery from trauma is not about forcing your body to do what it cannot do. It is about slowly, carefully, respectfully teaching your nervous system that new possibilities exist.

Start with the peripheral. Do not look directly into someone's eyes. Look at their forehead. Their nose. Their chin. The space between their eyebrows. This activates the same social engagement circuits without triggering the threat response. You get the connection without the overwhelm.

Notice what happens in your body when you try this. Do you feel more relaxed? More present? Or does even that feel like too much? If it's too much, back up. Look at their shoulder. Their ear. The wall behind them. You can be in conversation without ever meeting someone's eyes directly.

I want to slow down here for a moment. Because what we're talking about isn't abstract neuroscience. It's your life. It's the way you freeze when someone raises their voice. It's the way your heart pounds when you hear a door slam. It's the way you can't fall asleep without checking the locks three times.

Dan Siegel calls this the "window of tolerance" - that zone where your nervous system can handle what's happening without flipping into fight, flight, freeze, or fawn. For people with complex trauma, that window can be razor thin. Sometimes it feels like there's no window at all. Just a wall.

But here's what the research also shows - and this is the part that matters. Neuroplasticity is real. Your brain can change. Not overnight. Not through willpower alone. But through repeated experiences of safety, of co-regulation, of being met where you are without being asked to be somewhere else. Your nervous system learned danger. It can learn something different. It just needs time, patience, and the right conditions.

Another approach is to practice with yourself first. Look at your own eyes in a mirror. Not for long. Just a glance. See what happens. Do you want to look away? Do you feel shame? Do you feel nothing at all? That data is useful. It tells you where your nervous system is right now.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No by Gabor Mate. He talks about how the suppression of authentic response, including the urge to look away, creates long-term health problems. Reading it helped me understand that my body's refusal to make eye contact was not a defect. It was a form of truth-telling.

You can also work with your eyes in a gentle, titrated way. Look at someone's face for one second. Then look away. Then look back for two seconds. Then look away. You are teaching your brain that you can control the duration. You can end the contact. You are not trapped.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine. It walks through how trauma gets stored in the body and how gentle somatic practices can release it. The chapter on the eyes alone is worth the price of the book.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You by Mark Wolynn. He explores how family history shapes our nervous system responses, including how we handle connection and eye contact.

The social engagement system can be rebuilt

Deb Dana is a clinician who works extensively with polyvagal theory. She describes the ventral vagal state as a "glimmer." It is not a permanent state. It is a moment of safety that you can learn to recognize and gradually extend.

One of the ways to access the ventral vagal state is through the muscles around your eyes. The orbicularis oculi, the muscles that create crow's feet when you smile genuinely, are connected to the vagus nerve. When you soften your gaze, you signal to your nervous system that you are safe. And when your nervous system registers safety, it becomes possible to tolerate more eye contact.

Try this right now. Relax your jaw. Let your shoulders drop. Soften the space behind your eyes. Let your gaze go a little blurry. Take a slow breath. Notice if anything shifts.

This is not a quick fix. It is a practice. Your nervous system did not wire itself for hypervigilance overnight. It will not rewire itself overnight either. But every time you choose a small, safe moment of connection, you are laying down new neural pathways. You are teaching your brain that eyes can be soft. That gaze can be kind. That you are not in danger anymore.

Stay with me here. This is the part that matters most.

You do not have to forgive. You do not have to forget. You do not have to become some radiant, healed version of yourself who makes effortless eye contact at parties. You just have to keep your own nervous system with patience and curiosity.

One day, you might catch yourself looking at someone without thinking about it. You might hold their gaze for three whole seconds before you notice. And then you might look away, not because you are afraid, but because the moment simply passed.

That is not a small thing. That is your body learning a new language.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored by Deb Dana. It offers exercises specifically for regulating the autonomic nervous system, including practices for the eyes and face.

Healing Journey Resources

  • When the Body Says No by Gabor Mate - A book that connects the dots between stress and illness
  • In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine - Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing
  • It Didn't Start with You by Mark Wolynn - If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in this
  • Anchored by Deb Dana - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
